commands.author: clawsweeper[bot]
commands.review_summary:
The branch hardens Codex dynamic-tool/schema handling across app-server payload reads, runtime schema projection, plugin SDK/config validation, MCP/provider schema normalization, and plugin cache keying.

Reproducibility: yes. for the review finding: PR-head source shows the descriptor context encoder maps both string "1" and BigInt 1n to the same scalar key fragment. I did not run tests because this was a read-only review.
commands.review_followup: - [P1] A narrow automated repair can fix the descriptor-cache scalar aliasing; maintainer review still needs to accept the broader fail-closed compatibility behavior.

target.title: fix(codex): quarantine unsupported dynamic tool schemas
target.body:
## Summary

- Harden Codex dynamic-tool registration so malformed runtime tool schemas are diagnosed and quarantined instead of failing assistant turn startup.
- Validate nested JSON Schema shape hazards that Codex rejects, including malformed schema maps, schema arrays, optional schema-valued keywords, `required`, property dependencies, and dynamic schema keywords.
- Preserve valid current-turn executable tools when only stale registered metadata for the same name is invalid.
- Inline local `$ref` schemas under MCP/tool-schema dependency and additional-item keywords so `$defs` are not stripped while dangling refs remain.
- Keep MCP loopback `tools/list` alive when a tool schema union contains malformed property-schema variants; malformed variants are ignored while later valid variants can still define the property.
- Preserve optionality when MCP loopback union schemas include boolean `true` branches instead of over-tightening merged `required` fields.
- Prefer later usable MCP union property schemas over earlier boolean `false` property variants so `tools/list` does not forbid valid arguments.
- Clean Gemini provider compatibility schemas inside schema-valued dependency and conditional branches so inspection and normalization agree.
- Clean shared provider compatibility schemas inside schema-valued dependency, conditional, tuple, and additional-item branches so xAI-style inspection and normalization agree.
- Make plugin tool descriptor cache keys stable and resilient to non-JSON-safe ambient tool context metadata.
- Reject non-JSON-compatible `defineToolPlugin` config and tool parameter schemas at the SDK boundary before they can enter metadata/runtime registration.
- Reject non-JSON-compatible plugin factory tool parameter schemas before they enter shared runtime tool lists or the descriptor cache.
- Validate tool-plugin authoring metadata read from the SDK symbol so forged entries cannot feed cyclic/BigInt schemas into manifest generation.
- Reject non-JSON-compatible plugin config schema validator inputs before recursive schema walking, cache fingerprinting, or TypeBox compilation.
- Reject non-JSON-compatible bundle-MCP draft-2020-12 schemas before recursive schema walking or TypeBox compilation.
- Reject non-JSON-compatible plugin config schema metadata before recursive normalization or metadata exposure.
- Omit non-JSON-compatible extension config schemas from merged config schema responses before clone/merge paths can throw.
- Sanitize circular, unreadable, and non-JSON-compatible Responses input payload trees before OpenAI/Azure request serialization.
- Sanitize Codex trajectory payloads so non-JSON-compatible synthetic tool metadata cannot crash trajectory recording or bypass redaction.
- Reject unreadable plugin host-hook JSON payloads without throwing from host validation.
- Ignore unreadable Codex MCP elicitation request/display fields without throwing out of approval bridging.
- Sanitize unreadable Codex preserved-context tool payloads so context-engine projection cannot crash on hostile synthetic payloads.
- Fail closed on unreadable Codex app-server approval routing, permission-summary, policy-rewrite, and command payloads.
- Tolerate unreadable Codex app-server protocol response/turn fields before schema validation.
- Ignore unreadable Codex app-server notification correlation fields before routing/logging decisions.
- Ignore unreadable Codex app-server projector hook routing fields before event projection.
- Ignore unreadable Codex app-server side-question notification/request fields before side-thread routing.
- Ignore unreadable Codex app-server prompt-report schema and notification fields before report hashing or runtime routing decisions.
- Omit unreadable Codex diagnostic tool parameter schemas before model-call diagnostic capture.
- Ignore unreadable Codex app-server user-input request and resolution fields before chat prompt routing.
- Ignore unreadable Codex app-server attempt notification-state params before watchdog and terminal-state decisions.
- Ignore unreadable Codex native subagent notification params/item/content fields before completion extraction.
- Ignore unreadable Codex native subagent task-mirror params/thread/status/item fields before task lifecycle mirroring.
- Ignore unreadable Codex native subagent monitor params/thread/spawn/item fields before child-thread and completion routing.
- Ignore unreadable Codex app-server JSON-RPC message params/result fields before request, notification, or pending-response dispatch.
- Ignore unreadable Codex app-server tool-progress and mirrored-history sanitizer fields before logging or image-payload sanitation.
- Redact Codex app inventory error data through catch-bounded reads so unreadable synthetic fields cannot replace the original refresh failure.
- Fingerprint unreadable, circular, and non-JSON-compatible Codex dynamic tool fields without crashing thread lifecycle decisions.
- Fail closed on unreadable Codex plugin detail app fields before plugin app exposure reaches thread config.
- Fail closed on unreadable Codex plugin list marketplace fields before plugin app exposure reaches thread config.
- Fail closed on unreadable Codex plugin activation list/state fields before migration can attempt `plugin/install`.
- Normalize unreadable Codex app inventory entries before ownership and app exposure decisions.
- Normalize unreadable Codex plugin install auth-app summaries before activation diagnostics.
- Fail closed on unreadable Codex Computer Use marketplace discovery before install attempts.
- Fail closed on unreadable Codex Computer Use MCP status tool fields after install.
- Fail closed on unreadable Codex Computer Use plugin detail before install attempts.
- Normalize unreadable Codex Computer Use plugin summaries before install-state decisions.
- Ignore unreadable Codex Computer Use marketplace-add names before marketplace discovery.
- Reuse one shared JSON-compatibility walker across SDK and runtime plugin boundaries.
- Report unreadable fields from the shared JSON-compatibility walker as deterministic validation errors instead of leaking getter/enumeration exceptions.
- Keep exec-tool failure logging bounded when command/env params have unreadable synthetic fields, preserving the original tool error and redaction policy.
- Report unreadable tool-result `content` / `details` fields as stable adapter errors instead of leaking getter exceptions from synthetic plugin results.
- Keep plugin lifecycle trace logging from replacing original lifecycle failures when synthetic trace details have unreadable fields.
- Tolerate unreadable plugin descriptor-cache context metadata while preserving deterministic cache keys for readable fields.
- Tolerate cyclic and BigInt provider runtime config metadata while preserving type-tagged cache keys that do not alias ordinary strings.
- Use a shared type-tagged plugin cache-key encoder for loader/provider cache inputs so non-JSON-safe plugin config cannot crash or alias cache entries.
- Rename legacy schema-warning test fixtures to synthetic `fuzzplugin` / `fuzz_move_*` names so the PR no longer keeps the reported third-party plugin name in current test content.
- Keep test fixtures synthetic (`fuzzplugin`/`fuzz_move_*`) instead of naming the reported third-party plugin.
- Move plugin tool metadata storage into a lightweight module so schema diagnostics can name plugin/tool ownership without importing heavier plugin runtime code.
- Out of scope: changing global plugin materialization policy or rejecting valid non-Codex schemas outside the Codex/doctor projection path.

## Linked context

Which issue does this close?

No linked closing issue.

Which issues, PRs, or discussions are related?

No linked related issue.

Was this requested by a maintainer or owner?

Maintainer-directed hardening/debugging work.

## Real behavior proof

- Behavior addressed: malformed runtime plugin tool schemas can make Codex reject dynamic tool registration before an assistant turn produces content.
- Real environment tested: AWS Crabbox Linux runners, provider `aws`, using the branch over current `origin/main`.

  - `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/agents/tool-schema-projection.test.ts src/flows/doctor-core-checks.test.ts src/plugins/tools.optional.test.ts extensions/codex/src/app-server/dynamic-tools.test.ts`

What regression coverage was added or updated?

- Core tool-schema projection tests for malformed nested keywords, valid tuple `items`, valid property dependencies, and synthetic tool names.
- Tool-parameter schema tests for `$ref` in `dependencies` and `additionalItems`.
- Codex dynamic-tool tests for quarantined invalid executable schemas, disabled-call response, and stale registered-only diagnostics that must not hide a valid executable tool.
- Doctor core check fixtures use synthetic `fuzzplugin`/`fuzz_move_*` names.
- MCP loopback regression for malformed union property schemas using synthetic `fuzz_move_delta`.
- MCP loopback regression for boolean union optionality using synthetic `fuzz_optional_delta`.
- MCP loopback regression for boolean `false` property variants using synthetic `fuzz_boolean_delta`.
- Gemini provider compatibility regression for unsupported keywords inside schema-valued dependency branches using synthetic `fuzz_move_delta`.
- xAI-style provider compatibility regression for unsupported keywords inside schema-valued dependency branches using synthetic schema/tool names.
- Plugin descriptor-cache regressions for stable field-order-independent context keys and cycle-safe non-JSON-safe context metadata using synthetic `fuzzplugin` / `fuzz_move_delta` names.
- Provider runtime cache-key regressions for cyclic/BigInt config metadata and string-vs-BigInt cache aliasing using synthetic `fuzzplugin` / `mockplugin` names.
- Plugin loader/provider cache-key regressions for cyclic, BigInt, unreadable, Date/URL, and custom `toJSON` plugin config metadata using synthetic `fuzzplugin` / `mockplugin` names.
- Existing active-tool schema warning, inventory, gateway protocol, embedded-agent, and doctor repair fixtures now use synthetic `fuzzplugin` / `fuzz_move_angles` names.
- Plugin SDK `defineToolPlugin` regressions for bigint and cyclic parameter schema objects using synthetic `fuzzplugin` / `mockplugin` names.
- Shared JSON compatibility regressions for unreadable object properties, array entries, and key enumeration, plus a Plugin SDK `defineToolPlugin` unreadable-parameter-schema regression using synthetic `fuzzplugin` names.
- Runtime plugin materialization regression for non-JSON-safe plugin factory parameter schemas using synthetic `fuzzplugin` / `fuzz_move_delta`, while preserving the valid sibling `fuzz_status` tool.
- Tool-plugin authoring metadata regression for forged SDK-symbol metadata using synthetic `fuzzplugin` / `fuzz_move_delta` names.
- Plugin config schema-validator regression for cyclic schemas and bigint enum values using synthetic `fuzzplugin` cache keys.
- Bundle-MCP draft-2020-12 schema-validator regression for cyclic schemas and bigint enum values.
- Plugin config schema metadata regressions for cyclic direct/toJSONSchema output and bigint direct schemas using synthetic `fuzzplugin` cache keys.
- Config schema aggregation regressions for function, symbol, and throwing extension schemas using synthetic `fuzzplugin` ids.
- Responses image payload sanitizer regressions for circular tool output, circular root input arrays, circular inline image fields, unreadable top-level/nested objects, and non-JSON-compatible primitive payload values using synthetic `fuzzplugin` names.
- Agent tool-definition adapter regression for unreadable synthetic `fuzzplugin`/`mockplugin` exec failure params, preserving the original error result and command/env redaction.
- Current-main type fixture guards for malformed Responses context windows, Synology Chat webhook writes, and Twilio speech-event narrowing.
- Codex trajectory regressions for cyclic, `bigint`, function, symbol, non-finite, redacted, and unreadable synthetic `fuzzplugin` tool metadata.
- Host-hook JSON validation regression for unreadable synthetic `fuzzplugin` payload fields.
- Codex elicitation regressions for unreadable synthetic `fuzzplugin` routing fields and approval display parameter values.
- Codex context projection regression for unreadable synthetic `fuzzplugin` preserved tool payload fields.
- Codex approval bridge regressions for unreadable synthetic `fuzzplugin` routing fields, permission-summary values, policy-rewrite payloads, command actions, and command array entries.
- Codex protocol-validator regressions for unreadable synthetic `fuzzplugin` response/turn fields before schema validation.
- Codex notification-correlation regressions for unreadable synthetic `fuzzplugin` routing and nested turn fields before active-run routing/logging decisions.
- Codex event-projector regressions for unreadable synthetic `fuzzplugin` hook routing fields before hook event projection.
- Codex side-question regressions for unreadable synthetic `fuzzplugin`/`mockplugin` completion, user-input routing, and retry/error fields before side-thread routing.
- Codex attempt-context regressions for unreadable synthetic `fuzzplugin`/`mockplugin` tool schema fields before prompt-report stats and stable schema hashing.
- Codex attempt-diagnostics regression for unreadable synthetic `fuzzplugin`/`mockplugin` tool parameter schema fields before model-call diagnostic capture.
- Codex user-input bridge regressions for unreadable synthetic `fuzzplugin`/`mockplugin` request and resolution fields before chat prompt routing.
- Codex attempt notification-state regressions for unreadable synthetic `fuzzplugin` notification params before watchdog and terminal-state decisions.
- Codex native subagent notification regressions for unreadable synthetic `fuzzplugin`/`mockplugin` params, item, and content fields before completion extraction.
- Codex native subagent task-mirror regressions for unreadable synthetic `fuzzplugin`/`mockplugin` params, thread, status, item, and agent-state fields before task lifecycle mirroring.
- Codex native subagent monitor regressions for unreadable synthetic `fuzzplugin`/`mockplugin` params, thread, spawn source, item receiver-thread, and agent-state fields before monitor routing.
- Codex app-server client regressions for unreadable synthetic `fuzzplugin`/`mockplugin` JSON-RPC message fields before dispatch, plus empty-message JSON-RPC error preservation.
- Codex app-server sanitizer regressions for unreadable synthetic `fuzzplugin`/`mockplugin` tool-progress and mirrored-history image payload fields before logging or image-payload sanitation.
- Codex app inventory error-redaction regression for unreadable synthetic `fuzzplugin` app/list error data fields.
- Codex thread lifecycle regression for unreadable synthetic `fuzzplugin` dynamic tool fields before durable tool fingerprinting.
- Codex plugin thread-config regression for unreadable synthetic `fuzzplugin` plugin detail app fields before app exposure.
- Codex app inventory cache and plugin thread-config regressions for unreadable synthetic `fuzzplugin` app ownership fields before plugin app matching.
- Codex plugin activation regressions for unreadable synthetic `fuzzplugin` install auth-app lists and unreadable `mockplugin` auth-app names before activation diagnostics.
- Codex Computer Use regression for unreadable synthetic `fuzzplugin` marketplace discovery before plugin read/install.
- Codex Computer Use regression for unreadable synthetic `fuzzplugin` MCP server tool fields after install.
- Codex Computer Use regression for unreadable synthetic `fuzzplugin` plugin detail fields before install.
- Codex Computer Use regression for unreadable synthetic `fuzzplugin` plugin summary fields before install-state decisions.
- Codex Computer Use regression for unreadable synthetic `fuzzplugin` marketplace-add names before configured-source install.

What failed before this fix, if known?

- Codex dynamic-tool registration could reject malformed active tool schemas before content was produced.
- Shared SDK/runtime JSON compatibility validation could throw getter/enumeration exceptions instead of returning deterministic non-JSON-compatible errors.
- Exec-tool failure logging could let unreadable param getters replace the original tool error and risk leaking getter text instead of preserving redacted failure logs.
- Broad changed-gate proof exposed current-main test-only type errors in malformed policy/webhook fixtures; those are narrowed in this branch so the gate can complete.

If no test was added, why not?

- Tests were added.

## Risk checklist

Did user-visible behavior change? (`Yes/No`)

Yes.

Did config, environment, or migration behavior change? (`Yes/No`)

No.

Did security, auth, secrets, network, or tool execution behavior change? (`Yes/No`)

Yes. Malformed Codex dynamic tools are now disabled instead of being advertised or crashing registration.

What is the highest-risk area?

Overly strict schema-shape validation could disable a valid runtime tool schema.

How is that risk mitigated?

The projection preserves valid JSON Schema forms covered by review, including boolean schemas, tuple `items`, `dependencies` string arrays, and schema-valued `additionalItems`; focused tests and changed-gate proof cover the touched surfaces.

## Current review state

What is the next action?

Review the draft PR and CI after the latest force-push, while fuzzing continues in follow-up commits.

What is still waiting on author, maintainer, CI, or external proof?

GitHub CI was freshly queued after the latest force-push. Latest focused and broad external proof is AWS Crabbox `run_e3764df2a83b` / `cbx_f7ade5de8056`, where the Codex Computer Use proof passed 22 tests and `corepack pnpm check:changed` passed on base `7275304793`; the branch is pushed at `d43455dd7a`.

Which bot or reviewer comments were addressed?

Local branch autoreview findings were addressed before opening: property dependencies, `additionalItems`, current executable tool preservation, tuple `items`, null schema keyword diagnostics, metadata import weight, and Codex-specific projection scoping.

The branch adds a no-real-messages preflight compaction retry path, broadens `openclaw sessions cleanup --fix-missing` to prune empty/header-only transcript rows, and updates focused docs and regression tests.

PR surface: Source +90, Tests +57, Docs 0. Total +147 across 6 files.

Reproducibility: yes. there is a high-confidence source and log-based reproduction path: create an over-budget session entry whose transcript is empty/header-only, then let preflight compaction return `no real conversation messages`. I did not rerun a live Discord API reproduction in this read-only pass.

target.title: fix(sessions): recover empty preflight compaction
target.body:
## Summary
- Treat the specific preflight compaction result `ok: true`, `compacted: false`, `reason: no real conversation messages` as stale session token metadata instead of a hard compaction failure.
- Reset the context snapshot to 0 and retry the prompt so an empty/header-only transcript can self-heal on the next inbound message.
- Extend `openclaw sessions cleanup --fix-missing` to prune empty or header-only transcript rows, while preserving both typed message transcripts and legacy role-only transcript rows.
- Document the repair behavior.

## Verification
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/run.overflow-compaction.test.ts src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/run.empty-error-retry.test.ts src/agents/command/session-store.test.ts src/config/sessions/store.pruning.integration.test.ts src/gateway/server.sessions.store-rpc.test.ts extensions/discord/src/monitor/message-handler.preflight.test.ts`
- real local cleanup proof command shown below

## Real behavior proof
Behavior addressed: an over-budget session entry with an empty/header-only transcript no longer bounces every message after preflight compaction returns `no real conversation messages`; cleanup also repairs the empty-present transcript case without deleting message-bearing transcripts.

Real environment tested: local macOS checkout, Node/tsx runtime, real temporary `sessions.json`, and real transcript files covering empty, header-only, typed-message, and legacy role-only JSONL shapes.

Exact steps or command run after this patch: created a temporary `sessions.json` with `empty-present`, `header-only-present`, `typed-message-present`, and `legacy-role-present` rows; wrote matching JSONL transcripts on disk; ran `runSessionsCleanup(... fixMissing: true ...)` in dry-run and apply modes; reran the focused embedded-runner, session-store, gateway session RPC, cleanup, and Discord preflight suites listed above.

Evidence after fix: copied terminal output from the local cleanup proof command:
```text
dry_run missing=2 after=2 emptyFlagged=true headerFlagged=true typedMessagePreserved=true legacyRolePreserved=true
applied missing=2 remainingKeys=legacy-role-present,typed-message-present
remainingFiles typed=true legacy=true empty=true header=true
```

Observed result after fix: `--fix-missing` detects the empty and header-only stale-token rows before mutating, then removes only those store rows when applied. It preserves both a modern `type: "message"` transcript and a legacy `role: "user"` transcript, which also fixes the failed `sessions.compact` CI shard where a valid legacy transcript was being pruned before compaction. The runner regression still passes, showing `no real conversation messages` preflight compaction retries instead of surfacing an error with `compactionTokensAfter: 0`.

What was not tested: a live Discord account reproduction was not forced; the core state-machine deadlock, cleanup repair path, legacy transcript preservation, gateway compact RPC path, and Discord preflight surface were verified locally with deterministic runner/gateway tests and real temp session-store files.

Fixes #87016.

If this PR is squash/reworked, please preserve author attribution or include:
Co-authored-by: Andy Ye <35905412+TurboTheTurtle@users.noreply.github.com>

target.title: Fix Discord reply typing lifecycle
target.body:
## Summary

Fixes Discord reply typing feedback so an accepted inbound Discord message uses one typing lifecycle from preflight acceptance through queued processing and reply dispatch, instead of starting a Discord-only early cue and later starting a separate dispatcher typing owner.

This branch is intentionally scoped to the accepted Discord typing lifecycle. It does **not** change public plugin SDK signatures, core reply typing defaults, or default unconfigured `message_tool_only` behavior.

## Latest release audit

I rechecked the latest release line before this update:

- Latest stable checked: OpenClaw `2026.5.26` (`v2026.5.26`).
- Latest prerelease checked: OpenClaw `2026.5.27-beta.1` (`v2026.5.27-beta.1`).
- Current `origin/main` checked: `13dcded7c8`.

The newer reply improvements reduce general Gateway/reply hot-path overhead and visible reply delivery latency, but they do not fix this Discord guild accepted-typing gap. In `v2026.5.26`, `v2026.5.27-beta.1`, and current `origin/main`, `shouldSendAcceptedDiscordTypingCue` still rejects guild contexts before sending accepted typing. The dispatcher path still creates typing later from the reply pipeline.

So this PR still improves Discord responsiveness for accepted guild messages, especially allowlist guild channels with `requireMention: false`, by starting native typing immediately after preflight accepts the message and keeping it refreshed while the reply runs.

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior or issue addressed:** Discord reply typing feedback can be delayed or split into `typing -> idle gap -> typing` after OpenClaw accepts an inbound Discord message, leaving users without immediate acknowledgement while processing is already underway.
- **Real environment tested:** Real OpenClaw + Discord setup using public OpenClaw release `2026.5.7` for the before-fix reproduction, with the PR #76091 patch used for the after-fix comparison.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch:** Reproduce the Discord reply flow on the public release, apply the PR #76091 patch, restart OpenClaw, then send the same Discord reply flow again and observe Discord typing feedback during processing.
- **Evidence after fix:** Recording: https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/81bd8a69-d2c3-46f2-a23a-40f00694d8f7. The first 24 seconds show the pre-fix delay; the portion after 24 seconds shows the after-fix behavior with PR #76091 applied.
- **Observed result after fix:** Typing feedback starts promptly after preflight accepts the Discord message and stays consistent during queued processing and reply dispatch. The visible long delay is removed.
- **What was not tested:** No new live Discord recording was captured after the latest compatibility cleanup. The existing recording covers the accepted typing delay behavior this PR still targets; the latest code changes only narrow cleanup and keepalive implementation details.

## Current branch audit

- Current PR head: `cd1a7d7adf`.
- The branch is merged with current `origin/main` (`13dcded7c8`).
- The projected diff is limited to Discord monitor lifecycle files and focused tests.
- The PR no longer edits `CHANGELOG.md`, plugin SDK docs, shared `createTypingCallbacks`, or shared `ChannelRunQueue` public API.
- Skipped queued-run cleanup is handled inside the Discord message queue wrapper instead of adding a public `ChannelRunQueue.enqueue(..., options)` contract.
- Carried Discord typing feedback keeps the existing periodic refresh behavior, so long replies do not lose the typing indicator after Discord's typing expiry window.

## Root cause

Discord had two independent typing owners for the same user turn:

- `message-handler.ts` sent an accepted-message typing cue after preflight, and published releases only allowed that cue for DMs.
- `message-handler.process.ts` later created the normal reply pipeline typing callback and started it from dispatcher `onReplyStart`.

That split could make users see `typing -> idle gap -> typing again` while OpenClaw was already working. In guild channels, the accepted typing cue could be skipped entirely, so users saw no native typing feedback until the later reply dispatcher path.

## Changes

- Add `reply-typing-feedback.ts` for a Discord reply typing feedback owner backed by shared `createTypingCallbacks` from the non-deprecated outbound SDK subpath.
- Treat accepted Discord preflight as the visible acknowledgement boundary: start the carried typing feedback immediately for accepted messages, including guild allowlist channels with `requireMention: false` and no bot mention.
- Preserve the explicit opt-out: `typingMode: never` still skips accepted typing feedback.
- Carry the same accepted reply typing feedback through the queued inbound job and reuse it in `processDiscordMessage`.
- Retarget the same feedback owner to the resolved delivery channel/thread once the reply plan is known.
- Keep that feedback owner refreshed during long replies, then clean it up when processing completes.
- Clean up accepted typing feedback and release Discord inbound replay keys when queued work is skipped after Discord handler deactivation.

## Validation

Current local validation on head `cd1a7d7adf`:

- `node scripts/test-projects.mjs extensions/discord/src/monitor/message-handler.queue.test.ts extensions/discord/src/monitor/message-handler.process.test.ts` (`103` tests passed)
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/agents/code-mode.test.ts src/auto-reply/reply/agent-runner-memory.test.ts` (`97` tests passed)
- `pnpm check:architecture`
- `pnpm tsgo:prod`
- `pnpm check:test-types`
- `pnpm lint --threads=8`
- `pnpm run test:extensions:package-boundary:compile` (`109` plugins compiled)
- `git diff --check`

GitHub Actions are rerunning on head `cd1a7d7adf`.

target.title: fix(native-hook-relay): prune stale bridge files on registration
target.body:
> **AI-assisted PR** — diagnostic instrumentation, repro analysis, and patch authoring were done in a co-working session with Claude (Anthropic) on top of Cowork, and with ARC (a local OpenClaw agent on my install) as a second pair of eyes. All real-behavior tests below were run by me against my own gateway. I understand what the code does and have reviewed every line of the diff manually.

## Summary

Fixes the root cause behind issues #87536 and #73723: every native tool call in a Codex-backed session can return `Native hook relay unavailable` because Codex picks a stale bridge registration file from a dead prior gateway process.

After a crash, `SIGUSR1` supervisor-restart, or any non-graceful shutdown, the file in `/tmp/openclaw-native-hook-relays-<uid>/<hash>.json` is left pointing to a dead PID and an unbound port. The current gateway's bridge then competes with these zombies in the directory, and Codex clients can pick a stale entry. Once a client locks onto a stale entry, every hook invocation for that session fails until the next clean restart.

## Fix

In `registerNativeHookRelayBridge`, before doing anything else, scan the bridge directory and remove any `.json` record whose owning PID is **dead** (or whose `expiresAtMs` has passed). Records owned by other **live** same-uid OpenClaw processes (e.g., a parallel `openclaw-beacon-gateway.service`) are preserved.

Total: +49 / −1 lines in `src/agents/harness/native-hook-relay.ts`.

```ts
function registerNativeHookRelayBridge(registration: ActiveNativeHookRelayRegistration): void {
  // Prune actually stale bridge files from prior gateway processes. The bridge
  // directory is scoped by OS user (uid) and is shared across all OpenClaw
  // gateways/profiles run by that user, so a record with a non-current PID is
  // NOT automatically stale — it can legitimately belong to another live
  // gateway under the same uid. Only prune records whose owning PID is dead
  // or whose expiry has passed; leave live foreign records alone.
  try {
    const staleDir = ensureNativeHookRelayBridgeDir();
    const now = Date.now();
    for (const name of readdirSync(staleDir)) {
      if (!name.endsWith(".json")) { continue; }
      const full = path.join(staleDir, name);
      try {
        const rec = JSON.parse(readFileSync(full, "utf8")) as {
          pid?: number; expiresAtMs?: number;
        };
        if (!rec || typeof rec.pid !== "number" || rec.pid === process.pid) {
          continue;
        }
        let pidAlive = false;
        try { process.kill(rec.pid, 0); pidAlive = true; } catch { pidAlive = false; }
        const expired = typeof rec.expiresAtMs === "number" && now > rec.expiresAtMs;
        if (pidAlive && !expired) {
          // Live foreign record from another same-uid gateway/profile. Preserve it.
          continue;
        }
        rmSync(full, { force: true });
        log.debug("pruned stale native hook relay bridge file", {
          file: name, stalePid: rec.pid, currentPid: process.pid,
          reason: !pidAlive ? "dead-pid" : "expired",
        });
      } catch { /* ignore unparseable / racing files */ }
    }
  } catch (error) {
    log.debug("native hook relay bridge dir prune skipped", { error });
  }
  // ... rest of function unchanged ...
}
```

## Real behavior proof

### Behavior or issue addressed

Every native tool call in a Codex-backed session returns `Native hook relay unavailable` whenever the bridge directory `/tmp/openclaw-native-hook-relays-<uid>/` contains stale `.json` records pointing to dead PIDs from prior gateway processes (after crashes, `SIGUSR1` supervisor-restarts, or non-graceful shutdowns). Codex clients pick a stale entry, the bridge HTTP server they try to reach is gone, and every PreToolUse hook invocation is blocked. This blocks `date`, `time date`, file reads, MCP memory queries, and any other native tool from agent turns until the operator manually cleans the directory and restarts the gateway. Same underlying mechanism as #73723. The fix also has to **avoid breaking the multi-gateway case** where the same uid runs more than one OpenClaw profile (caught in initial review): the new predicate preserves bridge records owned by other live same-uid processes and only removes records whose PID is dead or whose `expiresAtMs` has passed.

### Real setup tested

- OpenClaw `2026.5.26` running as a systemd user service (`openclaw-gateway.service`)
- Ubuntu 22.04 in WSL2 on Windows 11
- Node 24.15.0 (bundled with the gateway runtime)
- Codex plugin enabled with: `transport: stdio`, `mode: yolo`, `approvalPolicy: never`, `sandbox: danger-full-access`
- A real ARC-named agent operating over the Discord channel surface, with real Codex subagent tool invocations
- Live parallel `openclaw-beacon-gateway.service` running under the **same uid (1000)** on the same machine, sharing `/tmp/openclaw-native-hook-relays-1000/`. This is the exact "same-uid multi-gateway" topology the review flagged.

### Exact steps or command run after the patch

1. Applied the equivalent of this PR's source diff to the bundled `dist/native-hook-relay-AN6S_wz5.js` on my live install.
2. Restarted only `openclaw-gateway.service` via `systemctl --user restart openclaw-gateway.service`. Left `openclaw-beacon-gateway.service` running.
3. Confirmed `openclaw-beacon-gateway.service` still answers requests after the ARC gateway restart (its bridge record was preserved, not pruned).
4. From ARC over the Discord channel: ran `date`, then ran `date` again, then ran `time date`, with variable gaps (immediate, ~30 seconds, ~3 minutes) across multiple Codex subagent sessions.
5. Inspected `/tmp/openclaw-native-hook-relays-1000/` and `ss -ltn` before and after restart to confirm the zombie cleanup and live-record preservation.

### After-fix evidence

**1. Bridge directory snapshot — before patch (5 zombies + 1 live ARC + 1 live Beacon):**

```
$ ls -la /tmp/openclaw-native-hook-relays-1000/
-rw-------  pid=11601  port=37109   # dead from prior session
-rw-------  pid=17733  port=46593   # dead
-rw-------  pid=90952  port=38385   # dead
-rw-------  pid=211925 port=44387   # dead
-rw-------  pid=240009 port=43303   # dead
-rw-------  pid=243162 port=46523   # <-- live ARC gateway
-rw-------  pid=229591 port=18889   # <-- live Beacon gateway (different profile, same uid)
```

**2. Before patch, every native tool call from ARC was blocked (live terminal output via Discord):**

```
> run date
Command blocked by PreToolUse hook: Native hook relay unavailable.
Command: date

> run time date
Command blocked by PreToolUse hook: Native hook relay unavailable.
Command: time date
```

**3. After patch — prune output on first registration after ARC gateway restart (copied live from runtime log at `/tmp/openclaw/openclaw-2026-05-28.log`):**

```
pruned stale native hook relay bridge file file=084c22487544d61d08bb88f20d738f6b.json stalePid=240009 currentPid=243162 reason=dead-pid
pruned stale native hook relay bridge file file=2413c88dfc4ad1e4e37edfe66958a932.json stalePid=211925 currentPid=243162 reason=dead-pid
pruned stale native hook relay bridge file file=90c38fd2c272f3d6c9d03a346dce35f3.json stalePid=90952 currentPid=243162 reason=dead-pid
pruned stale native hook relay bridge file file=654149472e7cd9034900a8d2dd43661e.json stalePid=17733 currentPid=243162 reason=dead-pid
pruned stale native hook relay bridge file file=5835f8d6faece4fb32fcec4784cd25d3.json stalePid=11601 currentPid=243162 reason=dead-pid
# Note: Beacon's bridge record (pid 229591) was NOT pruned — process.kill(229591, 0) succeeded, so the record was preserved.
```

**4. After patch — native tool calls succeed across multiple turns and time intervals (copied live from Discord transcript):**

```
> run date
Thu May 28 02:55:15 EDT 2026

> run date
Thu May 28 02:55:19 EDT 2026

> run date
Thu May 28 03:18:41 EDT 2026

> run time date
Thu May 28 03:21:04 EDT 2026
real    0m0.001s
user    0m0.000s
sys     0m0.001s
```

Five consecutive native calls in one shell process passed. Separate Codex tool invocations across multiple runs also passed. `time date` composite commands passed. File reads and AgentMemory MCP queries from agent turns also passed.

**5. After patch — bridge directory state, dead corpses pruned + both live processes preserved:**

```
$ ls -la /tmp/openclaw-native-hook-relays-1000/
-rw-------  pid=243162 port=46523   # ARC gateway (current process that just registered)
-rw-------  pid=229591 port=18889   # Beacon gateway (live foreign record, preserved)
```

Beacon kept answering tool calls in `working-room` (the auto-joined Discord voice channel) without re-registration, confirming the live-foreign-record preservation works end-to-end.

### Observed result

Before this fix the failure was 100% reproducible after a `SIGUSR1` supervisor-restart or non-graceful gateway exit had left even one zombie record behind. After this fix the same test cycle shows zero `Native hook relay unavailable` responses across all native tool invocations, **and** the parallel Beacon gateway continues operating without interruption (its bridge record is not pruned by the ARC gateway's startup scan).

### Not tested

- I did not test on macOS or native Linux (only Ubuntu 22.04 in WSL2 with Node 24.15.0).
- I did not test the PID-recycling edge case where a previously-OpenClaw PID has been recycled by a non-OpenClaw process between two registrations; the predicate currently falls toward "preserve" in that case, and the record is cleaned the next time the recycled PID either dies or the `expiresAtMs` passes. This is the safe direction (false-preserve over false-delete).
- I did not add an in-repo unit test that asserts "live foreign record preserved, dead-PID record pruned" — the existing `native-hook-relay.test.ts` doesn't have a fixture for spawning a real foreign-PID record, and adding one expands the PR scope. Happy to follow up if maintainers prefer.
- I did not run the repo's full `pnpm build && pnpm check && pnpm test` matrix from the fork (30+ minute pipeline locally; CI will exercise it).

## Why this is safe

- **No public API change.** The function signature is unchanged; behavior is purely additive.
- **No effect on the happy path.** When the bridge dir is already clean, the loop is a no-op (~ms cost).
- **Live foreign records are preserved.** Same-uid multi-profile setups continue to work.
- **`process.kill(pid, 0)` is signal-free under POSIX** — it is a permission/existence probe only, it never interrupts the foreign process.
- **Errors are swallowed.** A racing read/delete or unparseable file just gets skipped — the registration of the current bridge proceeds regardless.
- **Idempotent.** Running registration multiple times produces the same final state.

## What this PR does NOT do

This PR is intentionally scoped to the defensive fix. The broader architectural question of whether `renderNativeHookRelayUnavailableResponse` should fail-open instead of fail-closed (suggested in #73723 and discussed in #87536) is a separate decision about safety contract and should land in its own PR with an explicit config knob and a maintainer discussion of the default.

I also have local-only patches that defer the dedup file delete by 200ms and suppress the timer-fired `handle.unregister` from the Codex plugin's run-attempt module; both are useful as defense in depth but are not strictly necessary once this prune lands, so they are not included here.

## Refs

- Closes the recurring symptom in #87536
- Addresses the same underlying cause as #73723
- Incorporates ClawSweeper review feedback on same-uid multi-gateway preservation (#87563 review comment)
- I checked `release/2026.5.27` (source of `2026.5.27-beta.1`) — this fix is complementary, not duplicative; happy to retarget the PR there if maintainers prefer

— Adam Houk, Applied AI Solutions
target.author: applied-ai-solutions-hub

target.title: [Bug] Feishu channel dispatch broken in v2026.5.27-beta.1 — TypeError: Cannot read properties of undefined (reading 'run')
target.body:
## Description

After upgrading OpenClaw from v2026.5.26 to v2026.5.27-beta.1, the Feishu channel fails to dispatch incoming DM messages to the agent session.

## Steps to Reproduce

1. Upgrade from v2026.5.26 to v2026.5.27-beta.1
2. Send a DM to the bot via Feishu
3. Observe the error in logs

## Expected Behavior

Feishu DM messages should be dispatched to the agent session and receive a reply.

## Actual Behavior

Feishu WebSocket receives the message but dispatch fails:

```
[feishu] feishu[default]: received message from ou_xxx in oc_xxx (p2p)
[feishu] feishu[default]: Feishu[default] DM from ou_xxx: message
[feishu] feishu[default]: dispatching to agent (session=agent:main:main)
[feishu] feishu[default]: failed to dispatch message: TypeError: Cannot read properties of undefined (reading 'run')
```

## Root Cause

API change from `core.channel.turn.run()` to `core.channel.inbound.run()`. In 5.26, the channel runtime had `channel.turn.run`; in 5.27-beta.1 this was renamed to `channel.inbound`. The Feishu plugin runtime context does not have `channel.inbound` properly initialized, causing `.run` to be undefined.

## Impact

Feishu channel completely broken for DMs in 5.27-beta.1.

## Workaround

```bash
npm install -g openclaw@2026.5.26
systemctl --user restart openclaw-gateway
```

## Environment

- v2026.5.27-beta.1 (a9aaeea), upgraded from v2026.5.26 (10ad3aa)
- Feishu mode: websocket, DM policy: open
- Ubuntu 24.04, Node.js v22.22.2

target.title: fix(agents): concatenate signature_delta chunks in transport stream
target.body:
## Summary

Fixes the root cause of permanent session corruption when using Anthropic models with extended thinking enabled.

The `anthropic-transport-stream.ts` (used by the embedded agent runner) was **overwriting** `thinkingSignature` on each `signature_delta` event instead of **appending**. Since Anthropic sends the thinking block signature across multiple streaming chunks, only the last chunk survived. The truncated signature was persisted to session JSONL, and all subsequent replay attempts failed with HTTP 400.

## Root Cause

```typescript
// BEFORE (bug): overwrites on each delta chunk
block.thinkingSignature = delta.signature;

// AFTER (fix): concatenates all delta chunks
block.thinkingSignature = (block.thinkingSignature || "") + delta.signature;
```

The correct implementation already existed in the same repo at `src/llm/providers/anthropic.ts:629-634` — this fix aligns the transport stream with that reference.

## Changes

- **`src/agents/anthropic-transport-stream.ts`** — One-line fix: append signature deltas instead of overwriting
- **`src/agents/anthropic-transport-stream.test.ts`** — Updated 2 existing assertions + added dedicated multi-chunk concatenation test
- **`test/proof/thinking-signature-real-proof.ts`** — Real-scenario proof exercising the patched code path + live API replay validation

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior or issue addressed**: When OpenClaw stores assistant messages with Anthropic thinking blocks, the `signature` field is truncated (only last `signature_delta` chunk kept). On replay, Anthropic rejects the invalid signature with HTTP 400, permanently bricking the session. Fixes #87574.

- **Real environment tested**: macOS arm64, OpenClaw 0.6.0-dev gateway, Claude Sonnet 4 via Anthropic API proxy, Node.js v22.22.1. The patched code path section exercises `createAnthropicMessagesTransportStreamFn` directly with crafted multi-chunk SSE events. The live API section uses real Anthropic streaming with `thinking.type: "enabled"`.

- **Exact steps or command run after this patch**:
```bash
cd /tmp/openclaw-src
git checkout fix/thinking-block-storage
export OPENAI_API_KEY="<api-key>"
export ANTHROPIC_BASE_URL="<anthropic-compatible-endpoint>"
node --import tsx test/proof/thinking-signature-real-proof.ts
```

- **Evidence after fix**:

Terminal output from the real proof script:

```
=== Patched Code Path Proof: createAnthropicMessagesTransportStreamFn ===

  This section exercises the ACTUAL patched transport stream function
  with a mocked fetch serving crafted SSE events.

  Calling createAnthropicMessagesTransportStreamFn()...
  SSE payload: 5 signature_delta events

  ✅ PASS: Transport stream produced a thinking block
  ✅ PASS: Transport stream produced a text block
  ✅ PASS: Thinking text captured correctly (55 chars)
  ✅ PASS: Signature equals concatenation of all 5 chunks (75 chars)
  ✅ PASS: Signature is NOT just the last chunk (old overwrite bug would produce "rX4uVnB9oI6mKpL")
  ✅ PASS: Signature length 75 === sum of chunk lengths 75
  ✅ PASS: All signature chunks appear in order (verifies append, not prepend/shuffle)
  ✅ PASS: Text block content preserved correctly

--- Patched Code Path: 8 passed, 0 failed ---

=== Live API Replay Proof: Thinking Block Signature Integrity ===

API Base: <anthropic-compatible-endpoint>
Model: claude-sonnet-4-6

Step 1: Sending request with extended thinking enabled...
Step 2: Parsing SSE stream...

Step 3: Validation Results

--- Event Summary ---
  message_start: 1
  content_block_start: 2
  content_block_delta: 1766
  content_block_stop: 2
  message_delta: 1
  message_stop: 1

--- Signature Delta Analysis ---
  Total signature_delta events: 1
  Chunk 1: 432 chars "Er8CCl0IDhABGAIqQBXVqq4oYahHsQ..."

--- Signature Comparison ---
  Full signature (concatenated): 432 chars
  Overwrite-only (OLD BUG):      432 chars
  Thinking text length:          127 chars

--- Assertions ---
  ✅ PASS: At least one signature_delta event received
  ℹ️  INFO: Only 1 signature_delta chunk received (proxy/Bedrock may coalesce chunks)
         Multi-chunk concatenation verified by patched code path section above.
  ✅ PASS: Signature captured successfully (432 chars)
  ✅ PASS: Thinking text was captured from thinking_delta events

  ℹ️  Single-chunk delivery — simulating truncation for negative proof

--- Replay Validation ---
  Sending replay request with the captured signature...
  ✅ PASS: Replay with CORRECT (concatenated) signature succeeds: HTTP 200
  Sending replay with TRUNCATED (old bug) signature...
  ✅ PASS: Replay with TRUNCATED (old bug) signature fails: HTTP 400 — confirms the bug causes API rejection

--- Live API Replay: 5 passed, 0 failed ---

=== OVERALL PROOF SUMMARY ===
  Patched code path: 8 passed, 0 failed
  Live API replay:   5 passed, 0 failed
  Total:             13 passed, 0 failed

✅ PROOF PASSED — signature concatenation fix verified
```

- **Observed result after fix**: The proof demonstrates three critical behaviors:
  1. **Patched code path proof**: The actual `createAnthropicMessagesTransportStreamFn` function is called with 5 crafted `signature_delta` SSE events. The output `thinkingSignature` equals the concatenation of all 5 chunks (75 chars), not just the last chunk (15 chars). This would FAIL without the patch (old behavior would produce only "rX4uVnB9oI6mKpL").
  2. **Positive replay proof**: A thinking block signature captured from real Anthropic streaming replays successfully (HTTP 200).
  3. **Negative replay proof**: A truncated signature (simulating the old overwrite bug) is rejected by Anthropic with HTTP 400 — confirming this is the exact mechanism that permanently bricked sessions.

- **What was not tested**: Multi-chunk `signature_delta` delivery was not observed from the live API endpoint (AWS Bedrock proxy coalesces chunks). Multi-chunk concatenation through the actual patched function is verified by the patched code path section which feeds 5 separate `signature_delta` events directly into `createAnthropicMessagesTransportStreamFn`.

## Related Issues

- Fixes #87574
- Refs #80625 — Permanent session corruption from thinking blocks (this is the root cause)
- Refs #85781 — Claude signed thinking blocks poisoning session replay (P1)
- Refs #87475 — Invalid signature errors reaching users
- Refs #87329 — Subagent echo messages amplifying thinking block errors

target.title: fix(moonshot): rewrite duplicate native Kimi tool_call ids on replay
target.body:
## Summary

- Add a `preserveNativeKimiToolCallIds` option to the shared tool_call_id sanitiser that mirrors `preserveNativeAnthropicToolUseIds`: the first occurrence of a `functions.<name>:<index>` id passes through verbatim, later occurrences run through the existing occurrence-aware resolver and pick up unique rewritten ids.
- Plumb the option through `ProviderReplayPolicy` (public type), `TranscriptPolicy`, the embedded-runner replay sanitiser, and the per-message image sanitiser.
- Opt the Moonshot plugin in to the new behaviour by overriding `buildReplayPolicy` so OpenAI-compatible and Anthropic-messages Moonshot transports both keep native Kimi ids on first use and stop duplicating them on retry / long sessions / group chats.
- Out of scope: relaxing the `#62319` invariant (native Kimi ids must survive *first* occurrence) and changing tool_call_id generation upstream of the runner.

## Linked context

Closes #51593

Was this requested by a maintainer or owner?

No direct maintainer request. The issue is `bug:crash`, `P1`, `clawsweeper:fix-shape-clear`, `queueable-fix`, `source-repro`. The community discussion in the issue thread converged on \"Moonshot replay policy gap\" being the most likely root cause, which is the angle this PR fixes; ClawSweeper's own write-up also notes the existing occurrence-aware sanitiser is the right place to thread the new option.

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

- Behavior or issue addressed: Moonshot/Kimi returns native tool_call_ids shaped like `functions.exec:26`. Because `#62319` requires those native ids to survive replay, the Moonshot plugin disabled the replay sanitiser entirely. Long sessions / WhatsApp group chats reused the same `<name>:<index>` slot, replayed the same id twice in one request, and Moonshot rejected the second assistant turn with HTTP 400 `tool call id <id> is duplicated`.
- Real environment tested: Local OpenClaw source checkout on Linux with Node `v22.22.3`, pnpm `11.2.2` via corepack.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch: Ran a local OpenClaw source smoke against the registered Moonshot plugin via `registerSingleProviderPlugin` and the real `sanitizeToolCallIdsForCloudCodeAssist`, replaying two assistant turns that share the same `functions.exec:26` id. Re-ran after `git stash`-ing the patch.
- Evidence after fix:

  Before-fix (patch stashed):

  ```
  === BEFORE-FIX BEHAVIOR ===
  moonshot policy: {
    \"applyAssistantFirstOrderingFix\": true,
    \"validateGeminiTurns\": true,
    \"validateAnthropicTurns\": true
  }
  => policy has no sanitizeToolCallIds/toolCallIdMode => sanitizer never runs => duplicate native Kimi ids reach Moonshot verbatim
  ```

  After-fix (patch restored):

  ```
  === AFTER-FIX BEHAVIOR ===
  moonshot policy: {
    \"applyAssistantFirstOrderingFix\": true,
    \"validateGeminiTurns\": true,
    \"validateAnthropicTurns\": true,
    \"sanitizeToolCallIds\": true,
    \"toolCallIdMode\": \"strict\",
    \"preserveNativeKimiToolCallIds\": true
  }
  first toolCall id : functions.exec:26
  second toolCall id: functionsexec262
  first preserved as native: true
  second got unique rewrite: true
  first toolResult matches: true
  second toolResult matches: true
  ```

- Observed result after fix: First `functions.exec:26` survives verbatim (so #62319 stays fixed), the duplicate gets `functionsexec262` (unique, alphanumeric, length-9-ish via the existing `makeUniqueToolId`), and the paired `toolResult` ids match the rewritten ids by encounter order. The exact wire shape Moonshot would reject is no longer producible.
- What was not tested: A live WhatsApp group chat against Moonshot `kimi-k2.5/k2.6`. The fix is at the runtime replay sanitiser boundary that produces the wire-level ids, so the smoke exercises the exact transformation the live request would consume.
- Proof limitations or environment constraints: The smoke uses the real registered plugin and the real sanitiser in a local checkout but does not call Moonshot because this environment is not configured with Moonshot credentials or a WhatsApp instance. The reporter's HTTP 400 capture in the issue documents the upstream side of the same boundary.
- Before evidence (optional but encouraged): Captured above via `git stash`-ing this patch.

## Tests and validation

Which commands did you run?

- `PATH=\"\$HOME/.nvm/versions/node/v22.22.3/bin:\$PATH\" node scripts/test-projects.mjs src/agents/tool-call-id.test.ts extensions/moonshot/index.test.ts`
- `PATH=\"\$HOME/.nvm/versions/node/v22.22.3/bin:\$PATH\" corepack pnpm exec oxfmt --check ...` (all touched files)
- `git diff --check`

Outputs:

- `tool-call-id.test.ts` + `moonshot/index.test.ts`: `Test Files 1 passed (1)` / `Tests 5 passed (5)` for Moonshot, full tool-call-id suite plus the two new Kimi-specific cases all green.
- `oxfmt --check`: `All matched files use the correct format.`

What regression coverage was added or updated?

- `src/agents/tool-call-id.test.ts` adds two cases: \"preserves the first native Kimi tool_call id and rewrites duplicates\" and \"still rewrites duplicate native Kimi tool_call ids even when no preserve flag is set\" (locks the existing `#62319` invariant + the new dedup behavior).
- `extensions/moonshot/index.test.ts` updates the existing replay-policy assertion to reflect the new opt-in (`sanitizeToolCallIds: true, toolCallIdMode: \"strict\", preserveNativeKimiToolCallIds: true`) and adds an `anthropic-messages` case that asserts both anthropic and Kimi preservation co-exist.

What failed before this fix, if known?

The before-fix smoke above shows the Moonshot policy had no `sanitizeToolCallIds`, so the sanitiser never ran for Moonshot transports — that is exactly why the reported HTTP 400 could happen.

If no test was added, why not?

N/A.

## Risk checklist

Did user-visible behavior change? (`Yes/No`)

Yes (positive). Moonshot multi-turn sessions / group chats now survive duplicate native id replays instead of failing with HTTP 400.

Did config, environment, or migration behavior change? (`Yes/No`)

No.

Did security, auth, secrets, network, or tool execution behavior change? (`Yes/No`)

No (tool_call_ids are rewritten in-flight only; nothing is persisted differently).

What is the highest-risk area?

The new `preserveNativeKimiToolCallIds` propagation touches `ProviderReplayPolicy`, `TranscriptPolicy`, `replay-history`, and `images.ts`. A consumer that already supplied a literal policy object without the new key keeps the previous behaviour because the default everywhere is `false`.

How is that risk mitigated?

The default is opt-in: the new flag defaults to `false` in `DEFAULT_TRANSCRIPT_POLICY`, in `mergeTranscriptPolicy`, and in every existing call site. Only the Moonshot plugin sets it to `true`. Every other provider keeps its current sanitisation behaviour. The new `tool-call-id` test explicitly covers the off-by-default path so future contributors cannot accidentally regress the `#62319` invariant.

## Current review state

What is the next action?

Await maintainer / ClawSweeper review on (a) whether `preserveNativeKimiToolCallIds` belongs on the shared `ProviderReplayPolicy` or only as a Moonshot-local detail, and (b) whether the rewritten id format (`functionsexec262`) is acceptable to Moonshot's serving layer in practice — the alphanumeric+length-9 shape comes from the existing `makeUniqueToolId` strict mode and matches the `#62319` allowlist, but a live group-chat smoke would confirm it end-to-end.

What is still waiting on author, maintainer, CI, or external proof?

A live WhatsApp group-chat / long-session Moonshot smoke that exercises the duplicate-id path before and after the patch is still not provided from this environment.

Which bot or reviewer comments were addressed?

Initial PR; no PR comments yet. The patch implements ClawSweeper's recommended fix shape (\"enable occurrence-aware sanitisation while preserving native Kimi ids on first use\").

AI-assisted: yes. I reviewed the touched code paths, threaded the new option through the policy stack, and ran the focused validation commands above.

Made with [Cursor](https://cursor.com)

target.title: fix(native-hook-relay): prune stale bridge files on registration
target.body:
> **AI-assisted PR** — diagnostic instrumentation, repro analysis, and patch authoring were done in a co-working session with Claude (Anthropic) on top of Cowork, and with ARC (a local OpenClaw agent on my install) as a second pair of eyes. All real-behavior tests below were run by me against my own gateway. I understand what the code does and have reviewed every line of the diff manually.

## Summary

Fixes the root cause behind issues #87536 and #73723: every native tool call in a Codex-backed session can return `Native hook relay unavailable` because Codex picks a stale bridge registration file from a dead prior gateway process.

After a crash, `SIGUSR1` supervisor-restart, or any non-graceful shutdown, the file in `/tmp/openclaw-native-hook-relays-<uid>/<hash>.json` is left pointing to a dead PID and an unbound port. The current gateway's bridge then competes with these zombies in the directory, and Codex clients can pick a stale entry. Once a client locks onto a stale entry, every hook invocation for that session fails until the next clean restart.

## Fix

In `registerNativeHookRelayBridge`, before doing anything else, scan the bridge directory and remove any `.json` record whose owning PID is **dead** (or whose `expiresAtMs` has passed). Records owned by other **live** same-uid OpenClaw processes (e.g., a parallel `openclaw-beacon-gateway.service`) are preserved.

Total: +49 / −1 lines in `src/agents/harness/native-hook-relay.ts`.

```ts
function registerNativeHookRelayBridge(registration: ActiveNativeHookRelayRegistration): void {
  // Prune actually stale bridge files from prior gateway processes. The bridge
  // directory is scoped by OS user (uid) and is shared across all OpenClaw
  // gateways/profiles run by that user, so a record with a non-current PID is
  // NOT automatically stale — it can legitimately belong to another live
  // gateway under the same uid. Only prune records whose owning PID is dead
  // or whose expiry has passed; leave live foreign records alone.
  try {
    const staleDir = ensureNativeHookRelayBridgeDir();
    const now = Date.now();
    for (const name of readdirSync(staleDir)) {
      if (!name.endsWith(".json")) { continue; }
      const full = path.join(staleDir, name);
      try {
        const rec = JSON.parse(readFileSync(full, "utf8")) as {
          pid?: number; expiresAtMs?: number;
        };
        if (!rec || typeof rec.pid !== "number" || rec.pid === process.pid) {
          continue;
        }
        let pidAlive = false;
        try { process.kill(rec.pid, 0); pidAlive = true; } catch { pidAlive = false; }
        const expired = typeof rec.expiresAtMs === "number" && now > rec.expiresAtMs;
        if (pidAlive && !expired) {
          // Live foreign record from another same-uid gateway/profile. Preserve it.
          continue;
        }
        rmSync(full, { force: true });
        log.debug("pruned stale native hook relay bridge file", {
          file: name, stalePid: rec.pid, currentPid: process.pid,
          reason: !pidAlive ? "dead-pid" : "expired",
        });
      } catch { /* ignore unparseable / racing files */ }
    }
  } catch (error) {
    log.debug("native hook relay bridge dir prune skipped", { error });
  }
  // ... rest of function unchanged ...
}
```

## Real behavior proof

### Behavior or issue addressed

Every native tool call in a Codex-backed session returns `Native hook relay unavailable` whenever the bridge directory `/tmp/openclaw-native-hook-relays-<uid>/` contains stale `.json` records pointing to dead PIDs from prior gateway processes (after crashes, `SIGUSR1` supervisor-restarts, or non-graceful shutdowns). Codex clients pick a stale entry, the bridge HTTP server they try to reach is gone, and every PreToolUse hook invocation is blocked. This blocks `date`, `time date`, file reads, MCP memory queries, and any other native tool from agent turns until the operator manually cleans the directory and restarts the gateway. Same underlying mechanism as #73723. The fix also has to **avoid breaking the multi-gateway case** where the same uid runs more than one OpenClaw profile (caught in initial review): the new predicate preserves bridge records owned by other live same-uid processes and only removes records whose PID is dead or whose `expiresAtMs` has passed.

### Real setup tested

- OpenClaw `2026.5.26` running as a systemd user service (`openclaw-gateway.service`)
- Ubuntu 22.04 in WSL2 on Windows 11
- Node 24.15.0 (bundled with the gateway runtime)
- Codex plugin enabled with: `transport: stdio`, `mode: yolo`, `approvalPolicy: never`, `sandbox: danger-full-access`
- A real ARC-named agent operating over the Discord channel surface, with real Codex subagent tool invocations
- Live parallel `openclaw-beacon-gateway.service` running under the **same uid (1000)** on the same machine, sharing `/tmp/openclaw-native-hook-relays-1000/`. This is the exact "same-uid multi-gateway" topology the review flagged.

### Exact steps or command run after the patch

1. Applied the equivalent of this PR's source diff to the bundled `dist/native-hook-relay-AN6S_wz5.js` on my live install.
2. Restarted only `openclaw-gateway.service` via `systemctl --user restart openclaw-gateway.service`. Left `openclaw-beacon-gateway.service` running.
3. Confirmed `openclaw-beacon-gateway.service` still answers requests after the ARC gateway restart (its bridge record was preserved, not pruned).
4. From ARC over the Discord channel: ran `date`, then ran `date` again, then ran `time date`, with variable gaps (immediate, ~30 seconds, ~3 minutes) across multiple Codex subagent sessions.
5. Inspected `/tmp/openclaw-native-hook-relays-1000/` and `ss -ltn` before and after restart to confirm the zombie cleanup and live-record preservation.

### After-fix evidence

**1. Bridge directory snapshot — before patch (5 zombies + 1 live ARC + 1 live Beacon):**

```
$ ls -la /tmp/openclaw-native-hook-relays-1000/
-rw-------  pid=11601  port=37109   # dead from prior session
-rw-------  pid=17733  port=46593   # dead
-rw-------  pid=90952  port=38385   # dead
-rw-------  pid=211925 port=44387   # dead
-rw-------  pid=240009 port=43303   # dead
-rw-------  pid=243162 port=46523   # <-- live ARC gateway
-rw-------  pid=229591 port=18889   # <-- live Beacon gateway (different profile, same uid)
```

**2. Before patch, every native tool call from ARC was blocked (live terminal output via Discord):**

```
> run date
Command blocked by PreToolUse hook: Native hook relay unavailable.
Command: date

> run time date
Command blocked by PreToolUse hook: Native hook relay unavailable.
Command: time date
```

**3. After patch — prune output on first registration after ARC gateway restart (copied live from runtime log at `/tmp/openclaw/openclaw-2026-05-28.log`):**

```
pruned stale native hook relay bridge file file=084c22487544d61d08bb88f20d738f6b.json stalePid=240009 currentPid=243162 reason=dead-pid
pruned stale native hook relay bridge file file=2413c88dfc4ad1e4e37edfe66958a932.json stalePid=211925 currentPid=243162 reason=dead-pid
pruned stale native hook relay bridge file file=90c38fd2c272f3d6c9d03a346dce35f3.json stalePid=90952 currentPid=243162 reason=dead-pid
pruned stale native hook relay bridge file file=654149472e7cd9034900a8d2dd43661e.json stalePid=17733 currentPid=243162 reason=dead-pid
pruned stale native hook relay bridge file file=5835f8d6faece4fb32fcec4784cd25d3.json stalePid=11601 currentPid=243162 reason=dead-pid
# Note: Beacon's bridge record (pid 229591) was NOT pruned — process.kill(229591, 0) succeeded, so the record was preserved.
```

**4. After patch — native tool calls succeed across multiple turns and time intervals (copied live from Discord transcript):**

```
> run date
Thu May 28 02:55:15 EDT 2026

> run date
Thu May 28 02:55:19 EDT 2026

> run date
Thu May 28 03:18:41 EDT 2026

> run time date
Thu May 28 03:21:04 EDT 2026
real    0m0.001s
user    0m0.000s
sys     0m0.001s
```

Five consecutive native calls in one shell process passed. Separate Codex tool invocations across multiple runs also passed. `time date` composite commands passed. File reads and AgentMemory MCP queries from agent turns also passed.

**5. After patch — bridge directory state, dead corpses pruned + both live processes preserved:**

```
$ ls -la /tmp/openclaw-native-hook-relays-1000/
-rw-------  pid=243162 port=46523   # ARC gateway (current process that just registered)
-rw-------  pid=229591 port=18889   # Beacon gateway (live foreign record, preserved)
```

Beacon kept answering tool calls in `working-room` (the auto-joined Discord voice channel) without re-registration, confirming the live-foreign-record preservation works end-to-end.

### Observed result

Before this fix the failure was 100% reproducible after a `SIGUSR1` supervisor-restart or non-graceful gateway exit had left even one zombie record behind. After this fix the same test cycle shows zero `Native hook relay unavailable` responses across all native tool invocations, **and** the parallel Beacon gateway continues operating without interruption (its bridge record is not pruned by the ARC gateway's startup scan).

### Not tested

- I did not test on macOS or native Linux (only Ubuntu 22.04 in WSL2 with Node 24.15.0).
- I did not test the PID-recycling edge case where a previously-OpenClaw PID has been recycled by a non-OpenClaw process between two registrations; the predicate currently falls toward "preserve" in that case, and the record is cleaned the next time the recycled PID either dies or the `expiresAtMs` passes. This is the safe direction (false-preserve over false-delete).
- I did not add an in-repo unit test that asserts "live foreign record preserved, dead-PID record pruned" — the existing `native-hook-relay.test.ts` doesn't have a fixture for spawning a real foreign-PID record, and adding one expands the PR scope. Happy to follow up if maintainers prefer.
- I did not run the repo's full `pnpm build && pnpm check && pnpm test` matrix from the fork (30+ minute pipeline locally; CI will exercise it).

## Why this is safe

- **No public API change.** The function signature is unchanged; behavior is purely additive.
- **No effect on the happy path.** When the bridge dir is already clean, the loop is a no-op (~ms cost).
- **Live foreign records are preserved.** Same-uid multi-profile setups continue to work.
- **`process.kill(pid, 0)` is signal-free under POSIX** — it is a permission/existence probe only, it never interrupts the foreign process.
- **Errors are swallowed.** A racing read/delete or unparseable file just gets skipped — the registration of the current bridge proceeds regardless.
- **Idempotent.** Running registration multiple times produces the same final state.

## What this PR does NOT do

This PR is intentionally scoped to the defensive fix. The broader architectural question of whether `renderNativeHookRelayUnavailableResponse` should fail-open instead of fail-closed (suggested in #73723 and discussed in #87536) is a separate decision about safety contract and should land in its own PR with an explicit config knob and a maintainer discussion of the default.

I also have local-only patches that defer the dedup file delete by 200ms and suppress the timer-fired `handle.unregister` from the Codex plugin's run-attempt module; both are useful as defense in depth but are not strictly necessary once this prune lands, so they are not included here.

## Refs

- Closes the recurring symptom in #87536
- Addresses the same underlying cause as #73723
- Incorporates ClawSweeper review feedback on same-uid multi-gateway preservation (#87563 review comment)
- I checked `release/2026.5.27` (source of `2026.5.27-beta.1`) — this fix is complementary, not duplicative; happy to retarget the PR there if maintainers prefer

— Adam Houk, Applied AI Solutions

target.title: fix(diagnostics): wire memoryPressureThresholds config to diagnostic memory monitor
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: `emitDiagnosticMemorySample` accepts a `thresholds` parameter but no caller passes one, so the four hardcoded defaults (RSS warn 1.5GB/crit 3GB, heap warn 1GB/crit 2GB) cannot be tuned without patching the dist bundle.
- Solution: Add `diagnostics.memoryPressureThresholds` config fields and wire them through the heartbeat to `emitDiagnosticMemorySample`.
- What changed: Added `rssWarningBytes`, `rssCriticalBytes`, `heapUsedWarningBytes`, `heapUsedCriticalBytes` to the diagnostics config schema, types, help, labels, reload plan, and the heartbeat caller.
- What did NOT change: The default values and resolver logic in `diagnostic-memory.ts` are unchanged; the new config is entirely optional and defaults to existing behavior.

Fixes #87441

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior or issue addressed:** Users cannot override memory pressure thresholds through config; the four thresholds are hardcoded in `resolveThresholds` defaults.
- **Real environment tested:** Linux 4.19.112-2.el8.x86_64, Node v22, OpenClaw worktree at `/media/vdc/code/ai/aispace/openclaw-worktrees/pr-87551`. Gateway started on port 19002 with `diagnostics.memoryPressureThresholds.rssWarningBytes: 1024` (1KB — deliberately low to trigger pressure immediately on startup).
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch:**
  ```bash
  cd /media/vdc/code/ai/aispace/openclaw-worktrees/pr-87551
  OPENCLAW_HOME=/tmp/ocp87551-gw \
  OPENCLAW_GATEWAY_PORT=19002 \
  OPENAI_API_KEY=sk-... OPENAI_BASE_URL=https://api.deepseek.com \
  pnpm gateway:dev
  # Config: {"diagnostics":{"memoryPressureThresholds":{"rssWarningBytes":1024}}}
  ```
- **Evidence after fix:**
```text
[gateway] loading configuration…
[gateway] starting...
[gateway] http server listening (9 plugins: ..., 11.1s)
[gateway] ready
[heartbeat] started
[diagnostics/memory] memory pressure: level=warning reason=rss_threshold
  rssBytes=761544704 heapUsedBytes=582034584 thresholdBytes=1024
```
- **Observed result after fix:** Gateway started successfully with `rssWarningBytes=1024`. Within ~30s of startup, the heartbeat emitted a `diagnostic.memory.pressure` event with `thresholdBytes=1024` — exactly the custom value, NOT the hardcoded default of 1610612736 (1.5GB). This proves the config is wired through to `emitDiagnosticMemorySample` and the custom threshold controls when pressure events fire.
- **What was not tested:** Did not test sustained memory load to trigger the critical threshold. The warning-level event with a deliberately low threshold is sufficient to prove end-to-end config wiring.

## Regression Test Plan

- Coverage level: Unit test
- Target test file: `src/logging/diagnostic-memory.test.ts`
- Scenario locked in: RSS warning threshold override and heap warning threshold override each produce the correct `diagnostic.memory.pressure` event.
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: It covers the exact boundary where thresholds are applied before any diagnostic event is emitted.

## Root Cause

- Root cause: The heartbeat in `diagnostic.ts` called `emitDiagnosticMemorySample` without passing `thresholds`, and no config fields existed to populate them.
- Missing detection / guardrail: No config schema fields for memory pressure thresholds, and no test coverage for heap threshold overrides.

target.title: fix(agents): preserve reasoning_content replay across DeepSeek tier suffixes
target.body:
## Summary

- Strip well-known tier suffixes (`-free`, `-paid`, `-trial`) when building the `REASONING_CONTENT_REPLAY_MODEL_IDS` allowlist candidates so OpenCode Zen's `deepseek-v4-flash-free` (and any other tier-suffixed DeepSeek/MiMo/Kimi route) matches the base model id and keeps its DeepSeek-style `reasoning_content` replay.
- Out of scope: making `isDeepSeek` recognise OpenCode-native endpoints, adding an OpenCode Zen `wrapStreamFn`, or changing the replay sanitizer for non-DeepSeek-style providers. The minimal fix removes the 400 without expanding the replay contract.

## Linked context

Closes #87575

Was this requested by a maintainer or owner?

No direct maintainer request; the issue is `clawsweeper:fix-shape-clear` + `queueable-fix` + `source-repro`, and the reporter included a 4-step root cause analysis that points at exactly this allowlist gap.

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

- Behavior or issue addressed: OpenCode Zen exposes DeepSeek V4 Flash Free as `deepseek-v4-flash-free`. The shared OpenAI-compatible replay sanitizer used the bare allowlist (`deepseek-v4-flash`, etc.) so the tier-suffixed id never matched. The follow-up request to DeepSeek therefore had `reasoning_content` stripped from the assistant turn and DeepSeek rejected it with `400: \`reasoning_content\` in the thinking mode must be passed back to the API`, deadlocking the session.
- Real environment tested: Local OpenClaw source checkout on Linux with Node `v22.22.3`, pnpm `11.2.2` via corepack.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch: Ran a local OpenClaw source smoke against the real `buildOpenAICompletionsParams` with an OpenCode-routed `deepseek-v4-flash-free` model and a transcript that includes a thinking block carrying `reasoning_content`, then re-ran the same smoke after `git stash`-ing this patch on the same branch.
- Evidence after fix: Copied console output from the smoke.

  Before-fix (patch stashed):

  ```
  === BEFORE-FIX BEHAVIOR ===
  assistant.reasoning_content present: false
  assistant keys: [ 'role', 'content' ]
  bug repro: DeepSeek API would reject follow-up since reasoning_content missing
  ```

  After-fix (patch restored):

  ```
  === AFTER-FIX BEHAVIOR ===
  assistant.reasoning_content present: true
  assistant.reasoning_content value: \"Need to answer politely.\"
  ```

- Observed result after fix: The OpenCode-routed `deepseek-v4-flash-free` follow-up assistant payload now carries the original `reasoning_content` instead of being stripped down to bare role+content. The same matching path keeps preserving other tier-suffixed routes (MiMo `-free`, Kimi K2 `-free`).
- What was not tested: A live OpenCode Zen call against `https://opencode.ai/zen/v1`. The fix is at the sanitizer boundary that consumes the same OpenAI-compatible request shape; the smoke exercises that boundary directly using the model+context the live provider produces.
- Proof limitations or environment constraints: The after-fix proof uses the real shared sanitizer in a local checkout but does not call the OpenCode Zen endpoint because this environment is not configured with OpenCode credentials. The reporter's 400 capture in the issue documents the upstream side of the same boundary.
- Before evidence (optional but encouraged): Captured above via `git stash`-ing this patch.

## Tests and validation

Which commands did you run?

- `PATH=\"\$HOME/.nvm/versions/node/v22.22.3/bin:\$PATH\" node scripts/test-projects.mjs src/agents/openai-transport-stream.test.ts`
- `PATH=\"\$HOME/.nvm/versions/node/v22.22.3/bin:\$PATH\" corepack pnpm exec oxfmt --check src/agents/openai-transport-stream.ts src/agents/openai-transport-stream.test.ts`
- `git diff --check`

Outputs:

- `openai-transport-stream.test.ts`: `Test Files 1 passed (1)` / `Tests 204 passed (204)` — the existing reasoning replay coverage plus a new parameterised case for OpenCode Zen DeepSeek V4 Flash Free, OpenRouter MiMo V2 Pro Free, and OpenRouter Kimi K2 Thinking Free.
- `oxfmt --check`: `All matched files use the correct format.`

What regression coverage was added or updated?

Added an `it.each([...])` block under `buildOpenAICompletionsParams sanitizes reasoning replay fields` that asserts `reasoning_content` survives the replay sanitizer for three tier-suffixed model ids while still stripping `reasoning_details`, `reasoning`, and `reasoning_text`. The before-fix smoke above demonstrates the new assertions would not have passed without this patch.

What failed before this fix, if known?

The OpenCode-routed `deepseek-v4-flash-free` follow-up assistant payload had `reasoning_content` stripped, so any DeepSeek API call after the first turn returned HTTP 400 and the session deadlocked.

If no test was added, why not?

N/A.

## Risk checklist

Did user-visible behavior change? (`Yes/No`)

Yes (positive). Tier-suffixed DeepSeek-style routes can now complete multi-turn sessions instead of failing the second request with HTTP 400.

Did config, environment, or migration behavior change? (`Yes/No`)

No.

Did security, auth, secrets, network, or tool execution behavior change? (`Yes/No`)

No.

What is the highest-risk area?

A provider that ships a model id ending in `-free`/`-paid`/`-trial` that does *not* honour the DeepSeek-style replay contract would now receive `reasoning_content` it did not expect.

How is that risk mitigated?

The base id (after suffix strip) still has to be in `REASONING_CONTENT_REPLAY_MODEL_IDS`, which only lists DeepSeek V4, Kimi/MiMo, and friends that all document the same replay contract. The suffix list is intentionally narrow (three known tier markers) and only applied to allowlist candidate matching — the wire-level sanitizer behavior is unchanged for any base id that is not on the allowlist.

## Current review state

What is the next action?

Await maintainer / ClawSweeper review on (a) whether suffix-stripping is preferred over enumerating every `-free` variant in the allowlist, and (b) whether the OpenCode Zen plugin should also wire `wrapStreamFn` for parity with `opencode-go`.

What is still waiting on author, maintainer, CI, or external proof?

A live OpenCode Zen call producing the 400 → success transition is still not provided from this environment.

Which bot or reviewer comments were addressed?

Initial PR; no PR comments yet.

AI-assisted: yes. I reviewed the touched code paths, designed the candidate-stripping change, and ran the focused validation commands above.

Made with [Cursor](https://cursor.com)
